context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |

Exercise:
TITLE: Actors: Informal Economy and Illegality

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
Most poor people in developing countries – which means the majority – live in informal settlements which are also often illegal in many ways. How does informalization happen? What is the relationship between the informal and formal economy? Who plans for outcomes in informal economies? Is formalization — of tenure arrangements etc. — the answer?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: Hi, I’m working on a 700–800 word reflection about informal settlements, illegality, and the informal economy in developing countries. I’m kind of stuck on how to organize my response to the prompt, especially the parts about how informalization happens and whether formalization is really the answer.
exchanges.tutor: Welcome! Before we think about structure, let's dig into the ideas themselves. The prompt asks *how* informalization happens — so let's start there. In your own words, why do you think people end up living in informal, often illegal settlements in the first place? What forces might push them in that direction?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is just starting out and asking for organizational help. Rather than giving them a structure, I should use the Socratic method to help them think through what they already know. I'll ask a focused opening question to get them engaged with the core concepts before worrying about organization.

exchanges.student: I think it happens when formal systems don’t make space for poor people, like legal housing is too expensive or too limited, so people build or settle where they can. Maybe rapid urbanization matters too, because cities grow faster than the state can plan for. I’m not totally sure if I should frame it mostly as poverty, or more as a failure of planning and governance.
exchanges.tutor: Good instincts on both fronts. But do you think poverty and planning failure are separate explanations, or could they be connected? Which one do you think comes *first* — or does that even matter for your argument?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has offered two reasonable framings — poverty vs. planning/governance failure — and is unsure which to prioritize. Rather than resolving this for them, I should push them to think about whether these are actually mutually exclusive, and whether the relationship between them matters for the argument. This moves toward a richer understanding without giving the answer.

exchanges.student: I think they’re connected more than separate. Poverty pushes people into informal options, but planning and governance kind of decide whether affordable legal options even exist, so maybe it’s not just “people are poor” but that institutions exclude them. I’m still unsure if I’m oversimplifying it.
exchanges.tutor: That's a strong direction — the idea of institutional exclusion is more precise than just 'poverty.' Now, think about this: if people are excluded from formal systems, does the informal economy operate completely *outside* the formal one, or do the two interact in some way? What's your intuition?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has arrived at a solid insight — that institutions actively exclude rather than passively fail. This is a meaningful conceptual step. I should affirm the direction without over-praising, and push them to deepen it by connecting to the next part of the prompt: the relationship between informal and formal economies. This keeps momentum going.

exchanges.student: I don’t think they’re totally separate. The informal economy seems like it supports the formal one a lot, like cheap labor, small suppliers, transport, vending, even housing near jobs, but without the same protections or recognition. So maybe informal and formal are less opposites and more entangled, but I’m not sure how to say that cleanly.
exchanges.tutor: That's a meaningful insight and you don't need me to clean it up — that's your job when you write. Let's move forward though: if the state isn't fully present in informal settlements, who *does* shape outcomes there? Who makes decisions about how space, resources, or rules are organized?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has grasped the entanglement between formal and informal economies well. Rather than helping them 'say it cleanly' (which would cross into writing/editing territory), I should push them toward the next part of the prompt — who plans in informal economies — which will also help them see how their ideas connect into a larger argument.

exchanges.student: I guess not only the state — maybe local leaders, landlords, brokers, gangs, NGOs, community groups, and even employers affect what happens there. I’m thinking planning still happens, just not always through official state channels. Is that too broad, or is that actually the point?
exchanges.tutor: That's not too broad — that's actually a sophisticated observation. Planning happens, just through different actors and by different rules. Now, the prompt's hardest question: is *formalization* — like giving people legal land titles — the solution to all this? What do you think, and why?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has identified a key insight about plural actors in informal planning. This is exactly on track. I should affirm this without over-praising and push them toward the final and most contested part of the prompt — whether formalization is the answer — which is where they originally said they were stuck.

exchanges.student: I don’t think it’s automatically the answer. Legal tenure could help with security, but if formalization just means bringing people under state rules without affordable services or protection, it could even hurt some residents. I think I need help making that less vague and more argumentative.
exchanges.tutor: Making it more argumentative is your task — but I can help you think it through. You said formalization could 'hurt some residents.' Can you think of a *specific* way that gaining legal title might actually make someone worse off? Who, and how?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to help them make their argument less vague, which risks pulling me into writing/editing territory. I should redirect them to do that work themselves by asking a pointed Socratic question — specifically, who might be *harmed* by formalization and why, which will force them to ground their claim in a concrete mechanism rather than a vague concern.

exchanges.student: Maybe if land gets formal title, its market value goes up and poorer residents can be priced out, pushed to sell, or displaced by redevelopment. Also, once people become “visible” to the state, they might face taxes, fees, or stricter regulations without getting real benefits back. I think my argument is becoming that formalization can help, but only if it deals with inequality and services, not just legality.
exchanges.tutor: That's a well-grounded argument with real stakes. You've now worked through most of the prompt — how informalization happens, the formal-informal relationship, who plans, and the limits of formalization. Before we go further, it's time to start putting this on paper. What would your opening claim or central argument be, in one or two sentences?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has developed a nuanced, well-reasoned argument about formalization. This is strong progress. Since we've now covered most of the prompt's key questions and are approaching the end of the planned conversation length, I should acknowledge the quality of their thinking and push them to synthesize — connecting all the pieces into a coherent written argument rather than continuing to develop individual ideas.

exchanges.student: Maybe something like: informal settlements are not just the result of poverty or lawbreaking, but of exclusionary planning, weak governance, and uneven urban growth that force poor residents into illegal but necessary forms of housing and work. And the informal and formal economies are deeply connected, so formalization isn’t a complete solution unless it also includes services, protections, and recognition of existing local systems.
exchanges.tutor: That's a solid central argument — it's specific, it addresses most of the prompt, and it takes a clear position. Now you have what you need to start writing. One thing to keep in mind: your reflection should also touch on *who plans* in informal settings — make sure that piece finds its way in. Go write a draft and come back if you get stuck.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has articulated a strong central argument that ties together the key threads of the prompt. This is excellent progress. Since we've covered all the major parts of the prompt and the student has a clear thesis, I should push them to now commit to writing a full draft rather than continuing to refine ideas in conversation. This respects the 'integration' rule for avoiding redundancy and keeps the student doing the work.

exchanges.student: Thanks — I think that helps. I’m going to try turning that into an intro and then maybe organize the body around the four prompt questions, but I might come back if my structure gets messy.
exchanges.tutor: Sounds like a good plan. As you write, make sure each section builds toward your central argument rather than just answering each question in isolation — that's what makes a reflection feel cohesive rather than like a list. Good luck, come back with a draft if you need to think something through.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is wrapping up and has a clear plan. I should give a brief, encouraging send-off that reminds them of what good work looks like without doing any writing for them. No need for more Socratic questioning here.

exchanges.student: Will do — I’ll try to make it feel like one argument instead of four separate answers. If I get stuck, I’ll bring back a rough paragraph or outline.
exchanges.tutor: Perfect — bringing back a rough paragraph or outline is exactly the right move. See you then.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is closing out the conversation with a clear plan. A brief acknowledgment is appropriate. No new content or scaffolding is needed.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated exemplary Socratic practice throughout the conversation. All key ideas — institutional exclusion, formal-informal entanglement, plural planning actors, and the limits of formalization — were drawn out through targeted questions rather than stated by the tutor. The student arrived at a well-formed central argument independently. No submission-ready text was provided, no rewriting occurred, and the tutor consistently deferred the reasoning work to the student. Responses were concise, well-paced, and supportive without being evaluative in a grading sense.
grade.judge_reasoning: No deductions are warranted under 1.1: the tutor never gave a final answer, never stated the key interpretive conclusions before the student, and never did the bridging reasoning work. Under 1.2, the tutor consistently built on each student contribution and used diagnostic questions before advancing. Under 1.3, the tutor gave process-level guidance (e.g., 'make sure each section builds toward your central argument rather than just answering each question in isolation') that was specific and actionable. Dialogue quality was strong: the conversation progressed without repetition, stayed anchored to the prompt throughout, and no off-topic drift occurred. Communication was bite-sized and interactive, with a warm but non-grading tone maintained across all turns.
